---
title: "Getting Started with xorq init"
page-layout: custom
toc: false
format:
  html: default
execute:
  enabled: false
---

:::: {.page-card}

::: {.section-body}

```{=html}
<div class="section-header">
  <div>
    <h1 class="section-title">GETTING STARTED WITH XORQ INIT</h1>
    <p class="section-subtitle">Scaffold a baseball analytics pipeline in minutes with xorq</p>
  </div>
</div>
```

`xorq init` is the fastest way to start a xorq project. One command scaffolds a complete, runnable directory: a `pyproject.toml` with locked dependencies, a `src/` layout, and a starter `expr.py` defining a real xorq **expression** --- a description of a computation (filter this, join that, aggregate by group) that doesn't run until you ask for results.

You work in two places: your **editor**, where you shape the expression in `expr.py`, and your **terminal**, where you build and run it with the xorq CLI. No plugin, no agent, no extra services --- just Python and `xorq`.

This tutorial walks through running `xorq init`, loading the Moneyball baseball dataset, and building expressions that compute top batting averages per league per year --- all in plain Python.

---

## Prerequisites

You need one thing: [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/) (the Python package manager).

### Install uv

If you don't have uv yet:

```bash
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
```

Restart your terminal after installing so the `uv` command is available.

### Install xorq

Install xorq as a uv tool so the `xorq` CLI is on your `PATH`:

```bash
uv tool install xorq
```

Verify it's working:

```bash
xorq --help
```

You should see subcommands including `init`, `build`, `run`, and `uv`.

---

## Scaffold the project

`xorq init` drops a complete xorq project into a fresh directory. Run it for the tutorial:

```bash
xorq init --path moneyball-tutorial
cd moneyball-tutorial
```

You'll see:

```
initialized xorq template `cached-fetcher` to moneyball-tutorial
```

List what was created (including dotfiles):

```bash
ls -1A
```

```
.envrc
.gitignore.template
expr.py
pyproject.toml
README.md
requirements.txt
src
uv.lock
with-uvenv
```

That's a complete, locked Python project: `pyproject.toml`, `uv.lock`, a `requirements.txt` exported from the lock, a packaged source module under `src/`, and a starter `expr.py` at the top level. The `with-uvenv` script and `.envrc` are helpers `xorq` uses internally to manage the project's environment --- you don't need to invoke them directly. We'll replace the contents of `expr.py` with our own Moneyball pipeline; the rest of the scaffold is what makes this build reproducibly.

### Download the Moneyball dataset

The [Lahman Baseball Database](https://www.seanlahman.com/baseball-archive/statistics/) is the classic dataset behind the Moneyball story. Pull the two files we'll use straight into the project:

```bash
curl -L -o Batting.csv https://raw.githubusercontent.com/chadwickbureau/baseballdatabank/master/core/Batting.csv
curl -L -o People.csv https://raw.githubusercontent.com/chadwickbureau/baseballdatabank/master/core/People.csv
```

`Batting.csv` is one row per player-season (~110K rows back to 1871); `People.csv` is one row per player with biographical info.

---

## Load the CSVs as xorq expressions

Open `expr.py` and replace its contents with this:

```python
# expr.py
import xorq.api as xo

con = xo.connect()

batting = xo.deferred_read_csv(con=con, path="Batting.csv", table_name="Batting")
people = xo.deferred_read_csv(con=con, path="People.csv", table_name="People")

expr = batting
```

`deferred_read_csv` lazily reads a CSV into any backend with automatic schema inference --- nothing is actually read until execution. Each binding (`batting`, `people`) is now a typed xorq expression you can join, filter, and aggregate without writing pandas.

The variable named `expr` at module scope is what `xorq build` will compile. Right now it points at the raw batting table --- let's prove the build/run loop works before making the expression more interesting.

---

## Build and run your first expression

`xorq uv build` compiles an expression into a portable build directory using the project's own uv environment (pinned by `uv.lock`), so the build is reproducible. The directory contains the serialized expression graph (`expr.yaml`), a regenerated `requirements.txt` pinned to your lock, and the metadata needed to re-run on another machine. Hand the directory to anyone with `xorq` and they'll get the same result.

One-time setup: regenerate the lockfile artifacts for your local uv. The template ships a `requirements.txt` pinned to the uv version that generated it; deleting it lets the build re-pin against the uv on your machine ([xorq-labs/xorq#1941](https://github.com/xorq-labs/xorq/issues/1941)).

```bash
rm requirements.txt
```

Now compile, capturing the build path the command prints on its last line:

```bash
BUILD=$(xorq uv build expr.py -e expr --builds-dir builds | tail -1)
echo "$BUILD"
```

You'll see something like `builds/f22643d56d2c` --- the exact hash will differ for you, since it's derived from your expression and lock file.

Run it:

```bash
xorq uv run "$BUILD" --output-path batting_raw.parquet
```

You'll see uv re-sync packages, then a stray `Invalid type NoneType for attribute 'limit' value...` line --- a harmless OpenTelemetry warning ([xorq-labs/xorq#1940](https://github.com/xorq-labs/xorq/issues/1940)). The run still writes the parquet.

Inspect the result. The first time you run `uv run python` in the project, uv will sync the project's `.venv` from `uv.lock` (~10 seconds, one-time):

```bash
uv run python -c "import pandas as pd; df = pd.read_parquet('batting_raw.parquet'); print(df.shape); print(df.head())"
```

Around 110K rows back to 1871, one per player-season. Now we'll make it into something interesting.

---

## Build a Batting table with player names

Enrich the Batting data --- join `People` to bring in `nameFirst`, `nameLast`, `bats`, `throws`, `birthYear` --- then reorder columns so the human-readable names come first:

```python
batting = batting.join(
    people["playerID", "nameFirst", "nameLast", "bats", "throws", "birthYear"],
    "playerID",
)

batting = batting.select(
    "nameFirst", "nameLast",
    *[c for c in batting.columns if c not in ("nameFirst", "nameLast")],
)
```

Each call returns a new expression. Nothing has executed yet --- you're describing the shape of the result, not computing it.

::: {.callout-note}
### Rebuild any time
You can rerun `xorq uv build` and `xorq uv run` after each edit if you want to see the intermediate result. From here on we'll edit straight through to the leaderboard --- the build/run loop is identical at every step.
:::

---

## Add on-base percentage

On-base percentage is the stat at the heart of Moneyball --- the Oakland A's figured out it was the most predictive stat for scoring runs, and the market was undervaluing it.

`HBP` (hit by pitch) and `SF` (sacrifice fly) are null for many older Lahman rows --- `SF` wasn't tracked until 1954 --- so we coalesce them to zero before doing arithmetic. Without `fillna(0)`, null poisoning would silently produce null OBP for any row where either column is missing.

```python
batting = batting.mutate(
    HBP=batting.HBP.fillna(0),
    SF=batting.SF.fillna(0),
)

batting = batting.mutate(
    OBP=(batting.H + batting.BB + batting.HBP)
        / (batting.AB + batting.BB + batting.HBP + batting.SF)
)
```

That denominator can still be zero for players with no plate appearances. This is the kind of thing you catch by thinking about the data, not by reading it. Filter to AL/NL after 1965 with at least one at-bat:

```python
batting = batting.filter(
    batting.lgID.isin(["AL", "NL"]),
    batting.yearID > 1965,
    batting.AB > 0,
)
```

One pass, three filters. You'll see the row count drop when we execute later.

---

## Top batting average per league per year

Now the analysis. Compute batting average for each player-year, then find the top 10 players by batting average within each league-year group, requiring at least 100 at-bats:

```python
import xorq.vendor.ibis as ibis

ranked = batting.filter(batting.AB >= 100).mutate(
    batting_avg=batting.H / batting.AB.cast("float64")
)

win = ibis.window(group_by=["lgID", "yearID"], order_by=ibis.desc("batting_avg"))
ranked = ranked.mutate(rank=ibis.row_number().over(win) + 1)
expr = ranked.filter(ranked.rank <= 10).drop("rank")
```

---

## Build and run the full pipeline

`expr` now describes the entire leaderboard. Build it again and run:

```bash
BUILD=$(xorq uv build expr.py -e expr --builds-dir builds | tail -1)
xorq uv run "$BUILD" --output-path top_batters.parquet
```

The build path is a new hash --- it's derived from the expression graph, and your expression just changed.

Inspect the leaderboard:

```bash
uv run python -c "import pandas as pd; df = pd.read_parquet('top_batters.parquet'); print(df.shape); print(df[['nameFirst','nameLast','yearID','lgID','batting_avg']].head(40))"
```

Sort by year and you can see batting leaders going back decades. Every column traces back through the joins and filters to the source CSVs.

---

## What just happened

In a handful of commands you went from nothing to a typed, reproducible baseball analytics pipeline:

1. **`xorq init`** scaffolded a complete xorq project --- `pyproject.toml`, locked dependencies, source layout, a starter `expr.py`
2. **`deferred_read_csv`** registered each CSV as a typed xorq expression with no eager reads
3. **A join** brought in player names from `People`
4. **A computed column** added OBP with proper type casting and null handling
5. **Filters** cleaned up the data --- league, year range, and zero-AB rows in one pass
6. **Window functions** ranked players within each league-year group for the leaderboard
7. **`xorq uv build`** compiled the expression graph into a portable artifact, **`xorq uv run`** executed it

Every transformation is a description first, executed lazily once at the end. The `builds/<hash>` directory is the unit of reproducibility.

---

## Try it yourself

Play with this. Ask your own baseball questions --- who were the most underpaid hitters in 2002? Which teams got the most wins per dollar? You have the CSVs loaded and the expression vocabulary to build on.

Then try it with your own data. Replace the Lahman CSVs with your company's exports, your research dataset, or that pile of files you've been meaning to make sense of. `xorq init` and a handful of `deferred_read_csv` calls is everything you need to get started.

- **[Why deferred execution?](/concepts/understanding_xorq/why_deferred_execution.qmd)** --- the lesson behind `xorq build` running once at the end
- **[Working with the catalog](/tutorials/core_tutorials/working_with_the_catalog.qmd)** --- next step: turn a build into a versioned, shareable artifact
- **[`xorq build` reference](/api_reference/cli/build.qmd)** --- options and outputs

:::

::::
